mechanism: Destination-country visa expansion raises the expected return to the destination-relevant skill, inducing prospective migrants to enroll in training programs at origin.
Not all newly trained individuals migrate; those remaining add to the origin's resident skilled workforce.
Direct outflow of skilled workers reduces the resident skilled population (the brain drain channel). Often offset by skill_acquisition_response in the same study.
Training-supply elasticity caps how fast the skill_acquisition_response can scale; when private and flexible (Philippines, India), the response can dominate emigration outflows.
Remittances directly augment household disposable income and consumption.
Remittances relax credit constraints on schooling expenditures and shift household perceived returns to education.
Households allocate ~6% MPC of remittance income to health-care expenditures (Mexico evidence).
Diaspora reduces information/trust frictions; co-ethnic networks channel investment to origin.
Migrants connect exporters and importers across borders; ethnic networks enforce contracts and broker deals.
Migrant networks transmit ideas and codified knowledge from frontier destinations back to origin inventors.
Returnees bring foreign collaboration networks, raising co-authorship and citation impact for nonmigrant peers.
Return migrants improve management practices, governance, and technology adoption in firms they join.
Returnees transmit destination-country gender norms; liberal destinations transmit liberal norms, conservative destinations transmit conservative norms.
Returnees from democracies model and transmit democratic norms; diaspora resources support accountability demand.
Skilled outflows during recessions remove potential entrepreneurs and reduce new firm creation in the short run.
Short run: outflows raise labor costs and reduce productivity (EU enlargement evidence). Long run: firms adjust via capital substitution and technology adoption.
53-African-country IV evidence shows physician/nurse emigration does NOT increase infant mortality on net; within-country medical-worker distribution is the binding constraint.
Liberalized gender norms (delayed marriage, female autonomy) reduce fertility at origin.
